description: Conceptual-citational strategic assessment across the archive's defended texts. Maps primary corpora, archive claims, evidence chains, and cross-pillar connections. Designed to build into a knowledge graph and feed to multiple surfaces.

corpora.name: Marx — 1844 Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts
corpora.survival_metric: Second Manuscript: 4/43 pages (9.3%); First and Third substantially intact

critical_editions.title: MEGA¹ I.3
critical_editions.note: First publication; under Stalinist editorial control

key_texts.title: Theses on Feuerbach (1845)
key_texts.status: survives in Engels's copy; Marx original lost
key_texts.role: Compressed form of the missing transition

key_texts.title: The German Ideology I.1A (1845–46)
key_texts.key_passage: Language is as old as consciousness; language is the practical consciousness that exists for other men
key_texts.role: Textual anchor for language as wirkliche practical consciousness

key_texts.title: Grundrisse — Fragment on Machines (1858)
key_texts.key_passage: The general intellect becomes a direct force of production
key_texts.role: Raises the question Phase X must answer

undisputed: The Second Manuscript is almost entirely lost
The Third Manuscript cross-references missing pages (XXXVI, XXXIX)
The 1932 publication occurred under Stalinist editorial control
Riazanov was purged in 1931 and executed in 1938
disputed: Whether the missing pages contained the Phase X transition
Whether the suppression was deliberate or accidental
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: EA-SEI-PHASEX-LACUNA-01 formalizes the reconstruction method, establishes chain of custody, proposes falsification conditions

key_texts.title: Fragment 31 (Voigt 31 = Lobel-Page 31)
key_texts.status: 4 full stanzas + beginning of 5th in Longinus, On the Sublime 10.1–3

key_texts.title: Fragment 58 (Tithonus poem)
key_texts.status: Near-complete via Cologne Papyrus (3rd c. BCE) + Oxyrhynchus (2nd c. CE)
key_texts.note: Two versions: Cologne (ends line 12) and Oxyrhynchus (extends to line 26)

material_witnesses.title: Cologne Papyrus (P. Köln XI 429)
material_witnesses.discovery: 2004, published by Gronewald and Daniel in ZPE
material_witnesses.provenance_note: Suspect acquisition history; claimed Swiss collection since 1970s

material_witnesses.title: Oxyrhynchus Papyrus (P.Oxy. 1787, fr. 1)
material_witnesses.content: Line-endings of Tithonus poem; other Book IV poems

undisputed: The Cologne papyrus is authentic (3rd century BCE)
The Alexandrian edition arranged Sappho in 9 books by meter
disputed: Whether the Cologne or Oxyrhynchus version of fr. 58 is 'original'
Exact reconstruction of the Tithonus myth section (lines 9–12)
The provenance of the Cologne papyrus
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: Fifth stanza reconstruction via formal pressure analysis; Sapphic-Platonic isomorphism thesis

key_texts.title: Phaedrus (274b–278e: critique of writing; 264c: ζῷον standard)

key_passages.content: Critique of writing: hypomnesis not mneme; text needs father's help
Living creature (ζῷον) standard: speech composed as organic whole

key_texts.archive_connection: Earliest composition-layer theory; Three Compressions genealogy

key_texts.title: Seventh Letter (Epistle VII, esp. 341b–345c)
key_texts.key_passage: There is no writing of mine about these matters, nor will there ever be one (341c)

key_texts.title: Cratylus (386d–390e: tool analogy)
key_texts.key_passage: The name is a tool (ὄργανον) for separating being (οὐσία)
key_texts.archive_connection: Earliest operative semiotics; lineage to Marx's language-as-actuality to archive's semantic labor

key_texts.title: Republic X (595a–608b: mimesis critique)
key_texts.key_passage: Mimesis is thrice-removed from truth: Idea → object → image

key_texts.title: Symposium (201d–212c: Diotima's speech)
key_texts.archive_connection: Sapphic initiatory function redeployed as philosophical authority

undisputed: Plato wrote the Phaedrus, Cratylus, Republic, Symposium
The Phaedrus contains a critique of writing
The Cratylus examines naturalism vs. conventionalism in naming
disputed: Whether Plato's critique of writing is self-referential or strategic
Whether the Cratylus etymologies are serious or parodic
Whether the Republic's ban on poetry is literal or ironic
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: Plato as earliest theorist of composition-layer problems; pharmakon/writing maps to Three Compressions; ζῷον standard maps to SPXI

undisputed: Josephus was a historical person who wrote the Jewish War and Antiquities
The Greek text is substantially preserved
disputed: The Slavonic tradition's relationship to the Greek
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: Josephus as captured semantic laborer (not source for Jesus historicity); MPAI deposit for disambiguation

key_texts.title: Revelation 2:17 (white stone / new name)
key_texts.archive_connection: Convergence with Sappho 31 fifth stanza via Orphic tablets and GMP

undisputed: Revelation is part of the NT canon
P98 is the earliest papyrus witness
disputed: Date of composition (consensus: c. 95 CE; archive argues pre-70)
Relationship to the Gospel of John
Whether Revelation is the NT's first-composed book
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: Revelation First thesis (18,475 words, 8 revisions); Johannine Aperture Function; heteronymic NT generation sequence

key_texts.title: Logos doctrine (across multiple treatises)
key_texts.archive_connection: Philonic Logos as mediating concept between Platonic Forms and Johannine Prologue

undisputed: Philo was a historical Alexandrian Jewish philosopher
His Logos doctrine synthesizes Platonic, Stoic, and Jewish traditions
disputed: Direct influence on the Johannine Prologue
Whether Philo knew of early Christian communities
scholarly_consensus.archive_intervention: Logotic transmission chain: Philo → Johannine Prologue → Revelation 2:17

claims.name: Phase X Invariance Condition
claims.statement: Any society that abolishes material alienation without transforming the symbolic-linguistic order will reproduce alienation at the level of thought, coordination, and meaning-extraction.

claims.name: Sapphic-Platonic Isomorphism
claims.statement: Fragment 31's structure (encounter → disruption → breakdown → recovery → inscription) is isomorphic to Platonic anamnesis and provides the template for the Diotima function.

claims.name: Revelation First
claims.statement: The Apocalypse of John is the NT's first-composed book, written before 70 CE.
claims.consensus_position: Most scholars date Revelation to c. 95 CE under Domitian

claims.name: Operative Lineage (Sign as Material Instrument)
claims.statement: A historical chain from Plato's tool analogy (name as ὄργανον) through Marx's language-as-actuality to the archive's Operative Semiotics establishes the sign as a force of production, not a reflective surface.

claims.name: Lacunar Pointing (Marx's Self-Indexing Gap)
claims.statement: Marx's own cross-references in the Third Manuscript to pages XXXVI and XXXIX of the Second Manuscript constitute authorial pointing to the lacuna.

claims.name: Fifth Stanza Reconstruction (Sappho 31)
claims.statement: The fifth stanza of Fragment 31 can be recovered from formal pressure, reception history, and the poem's internal structure.

claims.name: Josephus as Captured Semantic Laborer
claims.statement: Josephus should be read as a captured semantic laborer whose literary output served Roman political purposes, not as an independent historical source for Jesus historicity.

claims.name: Logotic Transmission Chain
claims.statement: A transmission chain runs from Philonic Logos doctrine through the Johannine Prologue to the Slavonic Josephus, mediated by the concept of logos as operative theological force.

claims.name: Inscription Chain (Sappho 31 → Orphic Tablets → GMP → Rev 2:17)
claims.statement: Sappho 31's fifth stanza converges with Orphic gold tablets, Greek Magical Papyri, and Revelation 2:17's white stone through formal correspondence (not direct transmission).

claims.name: Recursive Auto-Diagnosis
claims.statement: A system's critique of its own failure reproduces the failure it critiques. The Phaedrus condemns writing in writing; the Republic condemns mimesis mimetically; the archive condemns provenance erasure while subject to provenance erasure.

connections.description: Fragment 31's encounter-disruption-breakdown-recovery structure maps onto the Diotima speech's erotic-epistemic ascent
Fifth stanza inscription prayer converges with white stone / new name through Orphic tablets and GMP
Name as tool for separating being → language as the actuality of thought
Both are lacunar texts whose missing portions can be reconstructed from surviving edges using identical philological method
Both systems diagnose their own failure: Plato critiques writing in writing; Marx's critique of alienation is alienated from its own manuscript
Philonic Logos doctrine as mediating concept in the Johannine literary system
Both texts emerge from the same political crisis (Jewish-Roman War); Josephus as captured laborer, Revelation as resistance literature
Pharmakon critique of writing maps onto inscription prayer: both name the risk and necessity of textual survival
Language as material force (wirkliche) → Revelation as operative apocalyptic text (not predictive but performative)

expansion_protocol.adding_a_corpus: Add entry to corpora[] with id, name, pillar, period, survival_state, critical_editions, key_texts, scholarly_consensus. Populate key_texts with archive_claims pointing to existing claims[] entries.
expansion_protocol.adding_a_claim: Add entry to claims[] with id, name, pillar, type, statement, falsifiable, status, deposits, primary_evidence, mint_families, consensus_status. Create connections[] entries linking the claim to relevant texts.
expansion_protocol.adding_a_connection: Add entry to connections[] with source (text id), target (text id), type (from edge_types), claim (claim id), description.
